data.title: Micron: The Peak Everyone Sees Isn't There
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) remains hot, as it is at the heart of the AI infrastructure development. Interestingly, change in the AI infrastructure continues to redefine the cycle and is gradually pushing Micron towards a more systematic outlook.

I emphasized the AI memory demand, high bandwidth memory ("HBM") strength, and margin expansion as the core pillars in my previous coverage. Indeed, these drivers have held firm, and conviction has enhanced with the course of time. Every earnings print, in turn, supports the same structural setup with increased visibility.

Additionally, Micron had an outstanding Q2 FY 25. Its revenues and gross margins ramped up. The quality of such growth was of equal importance as its scale. The AI-related memory did the heavy lifting and tilted the mix more to higher-value segments.

Despite this, the stock has not been on a smooth path since my last writing. After slipping into the $360-$370 range, it has found its footing again around $420 (as of writing). In fact, the market is striking a balance between good execution and the renewed cycle anxiety.

The price action simply means that the investors are resetting positions around their expectations.

Moreover, tools such as Google’s (GOOG, GOOGL) TurboQuant also bring up a concern that smart memory usage may alleviate the demand growth in the long run.

Nonetheless, the bigger picture looks pretty different.

The AI workloads continue to grow. Instead of decreasing the total demand, they consume efficiency improvements and convert them into even higher usage.

Memory supply will ultimately react at some point, and the cycles will come under pressure. Until that time, demand continues to outrun supply.

Overall, the current setup keeps supporting a Buy rating.

Demand is Structural Across the AI Stack

The idea of a peak cycle often comes from looking at Micron in isolation. However, the concept gets more meaningful when we begin with the AI stack and work backwards.

It starts with AI compute, transitions through networking and system design, and finally appears in DRAM and HBM demand.

For some color, let’s see what’s happening higher up the stack.

At the compute layer, Nvidia (NVDA) posted Q4 FY 26 revenue of $68.1 billion. It includes the data center revenue of $62.3 billion, up 75% YOY. At such a pace of deployment expansion, HBM and high-density DRAM scale with it. The reason behind this is pretty simple, as they are at the core of the AI memory bandwidth needs.

There’s the same momentum in custom silicon and networking, too. For perspective, Broadcom (AVGO) generated AI revenue of $8.4 billion in Q1 FY 26 at the platform level. It’s a whopping 106% upswing from Q1 FY 25. A boom in custom silicon and networking generally raises the amount of memory content per deployed rack.

Well, all of this flows back into Micron. Its core data center revenue rose over two-fold to $5.69 billion (sequentially), and operating margins clocked in at 67%.

Additionally, its Cloud segment brought in $7.75 billion in revenue with 74% gross margins, as compared to 66% in Q1.

Furthermore, Micron's Q2 .25 made it clear that this demand is no longer a forward-looking story. The revenue surged more than 3x YOY to $23.86 billion. Alongside, its non-GAAP gross margin crushed the guidance and rose to 74.9% from 56.8% in Q1.

This kind of margin expansion generally doesn’t happen towards the end of a phase. If anything, it appears more that demand is yet to catch up with supply.

The peak cycles usually exhibit cracks in demand visibility, but it’s not the case right now.

Currently, the revenue is growing faster, the margins are increasing, and the mix is becoming more AI-driven.

Efficiency Does Not Break the Demand Curve

Google’s TurboQuant poses a valid question for the memory cycle. It aims at the key value cache usage in long-context inference loads.

It can slash the memory needs by up to 6x and can deliver a speedup inference of up to 8x. On paper, this appears to be a huge headwind for demand.

When all requests become six times more efficient, it might be assumed that the pressure on memory vendors would eventually decrease.

Yet, the real system-level data points otherwise. The main driving force here is a mix shift, and companies like SK hynix (HXSCL) and Samsung (SSNLF) are already proving this.

Let’s put this into perspective.

SK hynix experienced tremendous growth in shipments of HBM, and the figures have increased by over 2x annually.

In addition, Samsung is anticipating an unprecedented Q1 FY 26 operating profit of 57.2 trillion won. It’s roughly 8-9x higher than last year and is largely due to its semiconductor business.

This means that memory per unit of compute is on the upward trajectory while software is becoming more intelligent.

Moreover, the Jevons Paradox is usually triggered by the efficiency gains in AI. When the price of a commodity such as memory footprint decreases by 6x, the usage doesn’t drop proportionally. The users would rather intensify their ambition. I can easily see this in context windows.

At the beginning of 2026, Gemini 3 Pro and Llama 4 Scout had already reached the 10 million token limit. These ultra-long contexts necessitate huge resident memory in the GPU, even when compressed.

This change is supported by the financials. Samsung has confirmed an upswing in its DRAM price in Q1 FY 26. Any space saved by decreasing the use of KV cache is consumed by these larger context windows and denser request batches. The system basically fills all the available headroom that is created.

After all, TurboQuant is a marginal adjustment within a system that continues to scale at a breakneck pace. I find the most obvious sign of it at Micron.

Recently, Micron forecasted a Q3 FY 26 revenue of $33.5 billion. This is higher than the annual revenue that it earned in nearly any year before 2025. Efficiency cannot outrun the sheer scale of the AI buildout, with its HBM production already sold out through 2026's end.

The cycle continues to be strongly propelled by the growth of computing power, with optimization being in the back seat.

Supply Risk is Real but Structurally Constrained

Following the Q2, investors paid a lot of attention to the decision to advance the fiscal capex by more than $25 billion.

This was despite the fact that the company guided towards another level of profitability for Q3.

That response takes the immediate fear that it is another peak on the rise.

Q2 also shows silently how this cycle is different. Margins remain robust as Micron moves to higher-value AI memory. That is important, since supply expansion in this cycle is all about creating more complexity-intensive products.

This is where the commodity system begins to fail, and HBM is at its center. It is based on 3D stacking and sophisticated packaging processes, which add actual yield and integration bottlenecks. That is, the output is not linear to capex, as in the case with the older memory cycles.

Such limitations go beyond Micron.

Another common choke point in AI supply chains is advanced packaging. The speed with which the ecosystem can grow is limited by capacity constraints at partners such as Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM).

The market is also changing in terms of its behavior as procurement is stretching out.

According to industry statistics, memory agreements are shifting towards 3–5-year arrangements. Micron is taking the lead in this change with its first 5-year Strategic Customer Agreement. This directly hedges demand visibility as opposed to exposing it to spot volatility.

The last and most crucial constraint is timing. The additions to supply are intentionally back-ended by Micron. Idaho output is projected in mid-2027, and the only year that Singapore's NAND capacity ramps is 2028. The near-term incremental supply is therefore pushed out.

The $1.8 billion acquisition of Powerchip fab is only predicted to contribute any significant DRAM wafer output from the second half of 2027. The lag continues to tighten the market until this year’s end.

In simple words, the cycle has become longer, more irregular, and essentially more predictable than any other era.

Valuation Discussion

Micron will no longer enter earnings with low expectations.

MU stock plummeted to roughly $377 a few days back, but then it climbed back again to about $420. The 12% move is more about positioning as opposed to fundamentals.

Basically, the market initially traded on uncertainty, then immediately retreated as the wider AI demand story held. This rebound narrows the margin for error.

Most of the heavy lifting is carried out by consensus estimates. The earnings are projected to rise aggressively. The FY 26 and 27 EPS estimates are trending towards the high $50s and $100, respectively.

The same is the case with revenue. This means a structural step-change and not a regular cyclical recovery.

Earnings roll over into 2027 and then start to normalize, which is why forward multiples will compress within a short period of time. Micron is currently trading at approximately 7x FY 26 earnings and a bit closer to 4-5x FY 27. This continues to add some level of apprehension regarding durability.

At about $420 today, the stock has already priced in strong earnings momentum, but it has not yet been fully re-rated.

My base case sits in the $425 to $430 range. This remains the most realistic near-term result. It presupposes a robust quarter and optimistic commentary on DRAM pricing and HBM demand. In such a case, Micron will not require a significant revaluation. It just has to hold its existing narrative and allow estimates to catch up.

That said, the bull case between the $440 and $460 range needs more than that. It’s required to have greater belief in the fact that supply constraints go further into 2026.

The last drop of $377 means that the buyers are ready to intervene quickly. However, it is also an indicator that much of the near-term optimism must have been pulled forward.

Micron is in a great spot fundamentally, but the stock is moving from a quick recovery into a phase where it needs to prove itself.

This is why I am remaining realistic with my base case at the moment. The bull case will begin to seem more likely as it will become apparent that earnings will only get better.

Risks to Thesis and Takeaway

The greatest risk in this case is the same at its core. Memory is still a cyclical business. The current cycle has been enhanced by AI, but it has not eliminated that nature.

History is a good guide. Micron has experienced steep earnings growth in previous cycles. Interestingly, it has an equal experience of sharp declines afterward when supply returns to equilibrium.

This trend does not really point to a collapsing demand. It just shows supplies returning to normal. So, in such a kind of situation, timing is more crucial than the direction.

Yes, the structure is robust today, but it remains vulnerable to changes in cycles. MU’s forward valuation already prices in much strength. The stock is trading at around 7x forward earnings. It is also close to 4.3x EV/Sales, as stability is already in the expectations.

The second risk is supply, though it is postponed. In Samsung and SK hynix, capex in the industry is increasing once again. This doesn’t affect the short-run pricing. That said, it would matter more for 2027 and beyond, when the new capacity will be introduced.

Furthermore, gains such as TurboQuant are currently absorbed by AI workloads with increased usage. However, efficiency ceases to be a tailwind and instead becomes a constraint to memory intensity when spending on hyperscalers decelerates. Such a change would directly affect the demand per workload.

Regardless of these risks, we have to count on positioning. Micron has a good underlying demand. This can be seen as it corrected sharply and then rebounded again to around $420.

Right now, Micron is no longer a secret success story, as the market already sees it. Now, the question is to what extent the AI memory cycle can run before supply catches up.

On the whole, MU’s valuation remains conservative, and earnings continue to go up. The opportunity lies in the gap.

The growth of earnings is continuing, and a complete re-rating is yet to come.

It’s sensible to stay with the trend if you are already holding. Meanwhile, if you are not entirely positioned, it appears like a decent entry point.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GPIQ: Yield Without Killing Returns
data.description:
Income ETFs with a covered call strategy work best when the market is range-bound. The underlying index should go up, but not by much beyond the strike price. Here, these funds own equities, sell options, and distribute cash. Looks simple, but what happens when the underlying performs better than expected and moves above the strike price? How do you retain some of that upside without giving it away to the option buyer? This is the key problem the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) is trying to solve.

In a typical covered call structure, let’s say the underlying index returns ~12-15% annually, and the fund also delivers ~8–12% total return – what happens to the remaining return? It goes to the option buyer — income ETFs that sell options sell some of your future upside for current income.

Here’s a quick look at the returns of a few income ETFs. They are all tracking more or less the same underlying companies, yet the outcomes vary greatly:

There’s a 2x difference between the worst and the best return. Yet, these names basically do the same thing. The difference is not in the stocks they select but in how aggressively they sell the upside. QYLD is a fully overwritten fund: it maximizes the option income but has a low equity return. JEPQ and QQQI are more flexible. They retain more upside, deliver better total returns (at least in this screen), and also offer good income.

GPIQ must be assessed as a part of this group. It is not just another high-yield ETF but an attempt to generate the highest income possible while retaining the most equity return.

What GPIQ Actually Is

Here are some useful metrics for GPIQ:

In some respects, GPIQ is closest to JEPQ and QQQI: double-digit yield, Nasdaq focus, and monthly income. However, the strategies differ. The GPIQ portfolio itself is essentially mega-cap Nasdaq, with a weighted average market cap likely in the trillion-dollar range, over 50% Tech exposure, and core holdings including the usual names — Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Amazon.

What’s most common is that all these ETFs start with highly volatile, high-growth equity portfolios. They use options, mostly selling calls on the Nasdaq, to generate additional income.

The key difference is that, unlike fully overwritten strategies, GPIQ does not sell all upside. Also, unlike JEPQ, it doesn’t use an ELN-based approach (ELNs come with counterparty risks, and income is taxed as ordinary income). It is closer to QQQI, generating income through option premiums and retaining a portion of equity participation. At 0.29%, its expense ratio is less than half of QQQI at 0.68% — subtle differences that add up to give an overall picture. From a rough calculation (what percentage of total index return is fund total return), we can get an idea that GPIQ retains ~95% of equity participation — for context, QYLD seems to retain only ~64%, while QQQI retains ~85% (taking QQQ's 1-year total return of 37.62%). This, to be very clear, is just a one-year snapshot and should not be taken as a model.

Where the Yield Comes From

What is actually funding GPIQ’s ~10% yield? From the few recent 19(a)s I checked, there are three sources: option premiums, the primary source; dividends from the underlying equities, a minor second source; and return of capital or realized gains, a variable but often prominent source.

Typically, for Nasdaq-100 heavy portfolios, underlying dividend yield is low, usually less than 1%. So the bulk of GPIQ’s payout doesn’t come from here. It comes from options and from realized gains. Nasdaq’s implied volatility (IV) is typically around 20-30%. This is what GPIQ works with, yielding around ~1% per month in favorable conditions. That aligns with its monthly and annualized yields.

Since GPIQ overwrites partially, it may earn less from option premiums, but it earns much more from equity gains. This shows up as realized gains and ROC. When funds have a strong realized gain component, ROC is often just an accounting artifact. For example, the fund may have sold upside via premiums or seen equity appreciations that have not been converted into taxable gains. So cash gets paid to shareholders now, which gets classified as ROC until those profits formally show up as realized gains.

Tax treatment is another advantage of GPIQ. Since a good portion of the distribution comes from option-related income, much of that falls under Section 1256. That means a larger part of these gains gets taxed as long-term gains – regardless of how long they were held. On top of that benefit, a second benefit is that a large share of distributions is classified as ROC, which gets deferred tax treatment and reduces cost basis.

Does the Structure Hold Up?

The investment case for GPIQ boils down to one question: after paying ~10% yield, is there anything left? If there isn’t, this is just another repackaged QYLD.

Starting with a Nasdaq-linked portfolio that has an expected gross return per cycle of roughly ~12-15%, and considering a GPIQ payout of around ~10%, that leaves us ~2-5% of residual return potential in a simple model scenario. This is the margin — if the strategy gives up more than that in upside, the total return structure collapses.

Here’s the scenario shown in the following table:

Return Mechanics — What Happens to $100 Invested by GPIQ

What we see from the above example is that, for GPIQ to work, they must harvest option income efficiently. They should not be selling too deep ITM nor overwriting. Upside participation must remain optimal. It should be able to at least partially capture rallies - this involves active management of the portfolio. The key target should be not to let NAV trend down over time; otherwise, distributions are just recycling capital.

Data suggests that QYLD fails the test because its high yield comes with weak total return and NAV compression over time. JEPQ and QQQI have done better, retaining upside while not destroying compounding potential. GPIQ sits in this group, with its ~10% yield comparable, sometimes slightly lower than, these peers, but its partial overwrite structure has been working better than QYLD’s. Its 0.29% cost is also efficient, slightly less than both JEPQ and JEPI and substantially lower than both QQQI and QYLD.

Note, though, that GPIQ doesn’t have the long track record of some of these other peers, so it needs to prove that it can consistently retain that 2–5% residual return.

Conclusion

Typical income ETFs aim to generate as much yield as possible. GPIQ aims to generate as much yield as possible while retaining some equity upside.

GPIQ is a relatively new product, so it doesn’t have a very long track record. So if you are buying GPIQ, you are betting on its ability to retain enough upside to justify giving up some of it for immediate income. While it pays ~10%, it needs to retain ~2-4% NAV growth, so it must stay in the ~12-14% total return range.

GPIQ is a good bet going into the earnings season because macro uncertainties may push implied volatility higher for Nasdaq options. As such, I consider GPIQ a buy going into an uncertain earnings season.

data.title: Tradeweb Exchange-Traded Funds Update - March 2026
data.description:
The following data is derived from trading activity on the Tradeweb Markets institutional European- and U.S.-listed ETF platforms.

European-Listed ETFs

Total traded volume

Trading activity on the Tradeweb European-listed ETF marketplace reached a record EUR 112.3 billion in March 2026, beating April 2025’s previous best performance by EUR 14.2 billion. The proportion of transactions and notional volume completed via Tradeweb’s Automated Intelligent Execution (AiEX) tool amounted to 95% and 27%, respectively.

Adam Gould, Global Head of Equities at Tradeweb, said: “March ETF activity contributed to a record first quarter for our European ETF platform, with volumes just shy of EUR 280 billion, significantly surpassing previous quarters. This elevated level of trading underlines how institutional investors are increasingly using ETFs to actively manage exposures and respond to shifting market conditions.”

Volume breakdown

Both equity and fixed income ETFs saw net selling in March, whereas ‘buys’ mirrored ‘sells’ in commodity ETFs. Activity in bond- and commodity-based products increased to 24% and 9% of the overall platform flow, respectively. Global Equities ETFs accounted for one-fifth of the monthly total traded volume, overtaking their North America Equities counterparts by EUR 2.4 billion.

Top ten by traded notional volume

Equity ETFs dominated March’s top ten by traded notional volume, reflecting trading activity across broad market and regional products. However, the commodity-based iShares Physical Gold ETC was ranked first. The ETF offers targeted exposure to the gold spot price via an investment in physical gold.

U.S.-Listed ETFs

Total traded volume

Total consolidated U.S. ETF notional value traded in March 2026 was a record USD 130.1 billion, up USD 11.6 billion from April 2025. AiEX transactions represented 56% of all U.S. ETF tickets on the platform, an all-time high.

Adam Gould, Global Head of Equities at Tradeweb, said: “Similar to Europe, March capped a record month and quarter for U.S.-listed ETF trading on Tradeweb. During the month, we saw sustained usage of fixed income ETFs, as investors actively repositioned around rate expectations and credit conditions. ETFs are clearly continuing to serve as a key liquidity and risk management tool in a more uncertain macro environment.”

Volume breakdown

As a percentage of total notional value, equities accounted for 59% and fixed income for 34%, with the remainder comprising commodity and specialty ETFs.

Top ten by traded notional volume

Half of March’s ten most actively traded ETFs were stock-based, with the Vanguard 500 Index Fund ETF (VOO) holding on to the top spot for the fifth consecutive month. The iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG) moved up two places from February to be ranked second.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: My Credit Market Message
data.description:
By Mike Larson

I just got back from our 2026 MoneyShow Masters Symposium Hollywood - and I’m still processing some of the incredible intelligence our speakers provided to attendees. Meanwhile, I shared a key credit market chart there... and what it “means” for investors and traders.

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day, which is from my opening day presentation. It shows the ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option Adjusted Spread - or in layman’s terms, how much extra yield higher-risk companies have to pay to borrow over and above what the US Treasury pays.

(Source: FRED)

Clearly, US companies are paying more to borrow money now - about 316 basis points over Treasuries versus 264 bps a couple months ago. But that’s nowhere near the levels we saw during the “Liberation Day” panic in 2025. Plus, the rise has been slow and steady this time around... rather than “spiky” as we saw back then.

That can be interpreted in two ways. The optimistic take is that things aren’t so bad (yet). Companies are paying a “war tax” - but it’s not catastrophic. The pessimistic view is that this looks like what you might see at the outset of a recession or bear market... versus a short-term crisis that has a better chance of fading quickly.

I’m still in the optimist camp - as I told our attendees at the Diplomat Beach Resort. But I’ll be watching credit. Closely.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: Prairie Operating: Reported A Loss For The Fiscal Year, Avoid
data.description:
The last article about Prairie Operating Co. (PROP) noted that it might lose more money. That article also covered the extreme amount of dilution that lies ahead. The dilution issue has been dealt with by management twice since the last article (at least). The last notice did note some progress on reducing the future dilution of shareholders. But I would still avoid this issue until the dilution issue is gone. Combine this with the fact that the company lost money for the fiscal year. There has got to be better choices for an investor than this issue.

Interim CEO

Another warning comes from the resignation of the CEO.

"On March 2, 2026, Edward Kovalik resigned as the CEO and Chairman of the Board of Directors (the “Board”) of Prairie Operating Co. (the “Company”), and Gary Hanna retired as President and director of the Company. The Board has appointed Richard N. Frommer, a member of the Board, to serve as Interim President and CEO of the Company, while the Company conducts a search for a permanent President and CEO."

This was noted in an SEC filing dated March 2, 2026. To me, this whole thing is worth reading because I thought that the senior officers, upon leaving, received a rather attractive separation package, given the fact that this company is not yet profitable and the stock price has not performed well at all.

Senior executives leave companies all the time. But in this case, they are leaving a company where investors will be severely diluted if there is any success and the company is not yet profitable. To me, this is a "straw in the wind" that there is more to come for investors (in the wrong direction).

Stock Price

The stock price action alone should be a cause for concern for anyone considering this investment.

The latest rise in commodity prices could well be what this company needs to straighten things out. However, those prices have to be helpful long enough to do the job. This is not an industry that is inclined to be run in that fashion.

Commodity prices are both volatile and low visibility. The commodity industry in general (really any commodity industry) moves very fast. The idea that a company that depends upon high prices for as long as it needs them will get them is quite a statement. The business is not run for any given company.

While any company that is in the situation shown above might succeed, generally not enough of them succeed to make this area of the market a viable place for investments. This is especially not for beginners.

Dilution

The dilution situation has definitely not improved since the last article.

Note that since the last fiscal year, there have been some dilutive issues that are now represented in common shares outstanding. In the meantime, there is a whole new dilutive entry shown above that is substantial.

Note that the common stock outstanding (fully diluted) has already nearly tripled. The latest report on dilutive securities shown before this slide shows very roughly another triple in shares outstanding.

This is going to cap any upside potential (if there even is upside potential to start with). In the meantime, before dilution, there is the outstanding debt plus preferred stock. That constitutes quite a bit of leverage for a new company with little to no operating history.

Keep in mind that this company essentially got going through an effective reverse merger. Therefore, the history of the company as currently constituted is not quite as long as the stock price history would indicate.

Successful Efforts Method - A Risk Factor Of This Method

One of the key considerations of using the successful efforts method of accounting is that there is no lower of cost or market calculation when using this method.

The reason for this (in the academic world) is that this was considered the more conservative method of accounting than the full-cost method. However, any system can be misused to the point that assets get overvalued. The lower of cost or market calculation was designed to prevent that from persisting when using the full-cost method of accounting.

However, the successful efforts method of accounting has no such guardrails. Instead, it depends largely upon conservative management choices to set up the system at the very beginning of a venture.

Most accountants are not trained in engineering (or anything close to that). What accountants can do is track how those assumptions are carried out. Therefore, unless there is an extreme situation caused by greed, some faulty estimates may never be caught (and frequently are not caught) until something happens (like bankruptcy or financial stress).

This is a risk of the successful methods effort, even though it was originally considered the more conservative method of accounting. It is extremely important to note when the CEO and President have resigned, the company is reporting a loss, and there is a lot of dilution ahead.

Summary

The combination of the resignation of key officers, a reported loss for the fiscal year, and the considerable dilution of shares makes this idea a very strong sell for the average investor.

Additionally, for me, the reported flow rates of wells drilled (given the location) appear to be on the low side. The absence of essential well costs and breakeven information further concerns me. It calls into question the assumptions used for the "Successful Efforts" method of accounting. Those questions affect the depreciation charge (is it correct, or should it be higher?). Right now, things do not make a lot of sense for me "all the way around" when it comes to possible company success.

The accounting choices are always an issue for a company reporting losses or a company that is highly leveraged (and key senior officers are resigning). Public accountants will tell you that they are one of the first parties to be taken to court when something goes wrong. Right now, there appear to be a lot of things going in the wrong direction.

For me, a confused mind "says no." I therefore watch from the sidelines until I can come up with a viable strategy (if that ever happens).

There is a lot going on here that screams this is not a successful investment idea. As long as that is the case, I avoid issues like this.

Risks

This company needs a sustained period of high prices to straighten out its balance sheet. It may need more high prices after that to get its costs down to competitive breakeven levels. It is really hard for me to see a successful investment idea given all the red flags that I see reviewing this issue.

Additionally, the risks of the successful methods accounting were discussed before in the context of the current situation. Normally, in the academic world, that is considered the more conservative method. However, it has also been used (commonly) in situations like this to aid the quarterly presentation favorability. Right now, it is very hard to tell what the situation is for sure. But put it down as a "straw in the wind" in the wrong direction given what else is obviously happening.

The key idea here is that the appreciation potential of the common seems to be capped by all the dilution as well as the current losses. Further warning for potential investors comes from the resignation of senior managers and the choice of the successful methods of accounting.

This could all turn out to be irrelevant because the company succeeds in becoming an operating company with a bright future. But for the time being, at least for me, it is not worth the risk to invest on that chance.

data.title: Brookfield: An Undervalued Compounder With 20%+ Annual Earnings Growth Potential
data.description:
Investment Thesis

One of the most valuable lessons I’ve learned in investing is that many of the world’s top investors tend to follow a similar playbook. A disproportionate number of “superinvestors” that I've studied focus on buying and holding high-quality businesses with durable competitive advantages that can compound earnings over long periods of time.

That observation changed the way I invest. I’m interested in deep value and high-growth businesses, but I particularly like exploring businesses that I believe can compound intrinsic value at above-average rates over the long term.

Brookfield Corporation’s (BN) track record of compounding, along with management’s continued focus on scaling fee-based earnings and permanent capital, makes it a particularly interesting company to study through this lens for a few reasons:

  • Management is guiding for earnings to grow at a 20%+ CAGR over the next 5 years.
  • The stock appears reasonably valued, if not cheap.

Management's 5-Year 25% Annual Earnings Growth Guidance

Brookfield's management is guiding for the company to see 25% annual growth in distributable earnings per share through 2030. I’ve followed many companies over the years, and this is an unusually bold target.

First, most companies don’t give long-term earnings guidance at all. You’ll typically see management teams provide guidance for the next quarter or the full year, but it’s reputationally risky for companies to provide guidance so far into the future. Additionally, most companies simply don’t have the kind of business model that affords the visibility needed to provide a meaningful 5-year vision for investors.

The companies that do provide longer-term guidance typically guide to mid-single-digit or low-teens growth. It's pretty rare for a company to call for a sustained 25% annual earnings growth. Management expects about 20% annualized distributable earnings growth from the core business, driven by asset management, wealth solutions, and operating businesses.

On top of that, carried interest and capital allocation are expected to add another layer, pushing total annual earnings growth from 20% to 25%. Carried interest is essentially performance-based income earned when Brookfield sells assets at a profit and takes a share of the gains. This can be lumpy in any given year, but across a large global portfolio, it becomes a meaningful contributor over time. Capital allocation is the other piece. Brookfield actively recycles capital by selling mature assets, redeploying into higher-return opportunities, and using its insurance float to fund investments at attractive spreads.

Together, these activities are expected to create incremental annual earnings growth of about 5% on top of the base business.

The Track Record to Back It Up

Brookfield has a more than 30-year track record of compounding capital, which helps support the credibility of its forward guidance. This track record is also uncommon across the broader investing universe.

In 2020, Brookfield laid out a 5-year plan to grow Distributable Earnings Before Realizations at roughly 19% annually and increase plan value at a 16% CAGR. Over the past 5 years, they exceeded it by delivering 22% annual DE Before Realizations growth and 16% annual growth in plan value.

That's the past 5 years. On a longer-term view, the stock has compounded at 19% annually, widely outperforming market benchmarks:

Brookfield stock only delivered about a 5-6% annual return over the past 5 years, with a 1% dividend yield, which I think is largely due to multiple compression over that period and the stock trading cheaply today.

The natural question at this point is, “How can Brookfield continue to compound earnings at this level?”

The company has been blessed in the past few decades with a near-ideal setup, starting from a small base and benefiting from tailwinds such as falling interest rates, rising asset values, and a boom in private markets. Competition was also less intense, with fewer scaled alternative asset managers competing for deals.

To me, the company’s stated goals of delivering 20%+ annual earnings growth and 15%+ compound returns for shareholders over the next five years don't seem too crazy.

The company now has two major growth engines that did not exist at the same scale historically:

  1. Brookfield Asset Management (BAM): A large, recurring fee stream from asset management
  2. Brookfield Wealth Solutions (BNT): A rapidly growing insurance platform that provides permanent capital.

These structural advantages could help offset the headwinds from size and competition.

Brookfield Asset Management (Core Segment)

A useful starting point for analyzing Brookfield is looking at its exposure to fee-based asset management through Brookfield Asset Management.

BAM represents the most predictable and scalable earnings stream within the broader business.

Brookfield’s 2025 Distributable Earnings by Segment:

  • Asset Management: $2.77B (46.0% of total)

  • Wealth Solutions: $1.67B (27.8% of total)

  • Operating Businesses (total): $1.60B (26.7% of total)

    • BEP (Renewables): $454M

    • BIP (Infrastructure): $356M

    • BBU (Private Equity): $24M

    • BPG (Real Estate): $737M

    • Other: $31M

  • Total: $6.04 billion in DE (before factoring in corporate costs, carried interest, etc.)

Q4 BN Supplemental

Fee-based income generally is less cyclical than other sources of income, less subject to market timing issues, and generally a better measure of the magnitude of capital. This type of revenue is supported by very lengthy capital commitments and, as such, provides visibility that is relatively rare for most companies involved in real asset management, as income may depend on the cyclical nature of their investments or transactions.

As of the end of 2025, Brookfield Asset Management had fee-based capital totaling approximately $603 billion, with fee-based earnings growing by 22% from the previous year to reach $3.0 billion per year. The second important consideration would be consistency in raising capital.

Having earned over $100 billion in revenue in 2025, it would be logical to say that there is consistent demand for their services from institutions. In the future, the company aims to maintain an inflow of over $100 billion annually, as is stated in their Investor Day Presentation:

This is important because continued fundraising is what sustains growth in fee-bearing capital, which in turn drives incremental earnings without requiring proportional increases in costs.

Taken together, this segment provides a clearer lens into the company’s underlying earnings power. It introduces a recurring, margin-rich component that differentiates Brookfield from many asset owners or operators, and it is likely to be the primary driver of long-term earnings stability if current trends persist.

Wealth Solutions (Growth Segment)

Apart from the asset management division, Brookfield is also working on building up a unique second source of income through its Wealth Solutions division, which primarily operates in the insurance and retirement products space.

Essentially, the company raises money from the policyholders with longer durations and invests the capital in high-yield securities, thereby gaining a differential between the payments made and the earnings from the investment. The management refers to this strategy as the creation of what can be termed a permanent capital float, just like Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) and Markel Group (MKL).

The Wealth Solutions division will be one of the major drivers of growth for Brookfield, contributing to the company's earnings growth of 34% per annum in the upcoming five-year period.

This is one of the key differences between Brookfield Corporation and its asset management arm. A pure asset manager like BAM relies on raising third-party capital and earning fees on it, whereas Brookfield, through Wealth Solutions, is increasingly generating its own capital internally. Internally generated capital is typically more stable and less dependent on market fundraising cycles.

This eventually becomes a self-fulfilling system whereby the asset management company manages funds, Wealth Solutions creates proprietary capital with a long duration, and Brookfield has its operating companies that can house the capital. As this system continues to scale up, it might lead to Brookfield being less reliant on raising capital externally.

Valuation

At the end of the day, when I’m evaluating a high-quality compounder business for the long term, like Brookfield, I would typically aim to pay a reasonable price rather than attempt to perfectly time entry points. In the meaningful (yet overused) words of Buffett, "It's far better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price than a fair company at a wonderful price."

Today, Brookfield appears to be trading at a pretty reasonable multiple. While Price/Distributable Earnings would probably be the best multiple to analyze the stock, P/FFO (Price/Funds from Operations) helps approximate the company's valuation while smoothing out the company’s earnings:

At 15.4x P/FFO, the stock is trading at a slight discount to its 10-year average.

While Brookfield’s diversified business mix makes it difficult to pick direct comps, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) and Digital Realty Trust (DLR) provide a helpful benchmark for how the market values similar real asset and infrastructure-focused businesses on a P/FFO basis.

Additionally, management estimated the company had a $68 plan value per share in its sum-of-the-parts valuation framework at the August 2025 Investor Day presentation. Compared to the stock's current share price of $41, this implies significant upside, although SOTP estimates are more directional by nature.

The Math of a "Fair Price"

Management is guiding for earnings growth at a 20%+ CAGR over the next 5 years. If the stock were to theoretically trade at a similar multiple in five years as it does today, that level of earnings growth would broadly translate into comparable annualized returns. In practice, realized returns will also depend on factors such as dividends, share repurchases, realizations (carried interest & capital allocation), sentiment, etc.

Even so, this framework highlights that if the business can compound earnings at a high rate (20%+ in this case), a large portion of long-term returns can be driven by earnings growth. Management has a stated goal of delivering 15%+ compound annual returns, which seems pretty reasonable if they deliver 20%+ annual earnings growth.

That’s why, for companies like Brookfield, my goal is to assess the company’s quality, and I think the multiple/valuation should generally take care of itself.

Risks

The biggest risk right now is that real estate and other asset-heavy sectors will keep bringing down overall results. If property values keep going down or leasing conditions stay bad, it could undo a lot of the progress made by Brookfield's higher-quality earnings streams and slow down the growth of distributable earnings.

Another risk is that Wealth Solutions doesn't grow as expected or makes less money than it says it will. The Wealth Solutions model relies on generating appealing returns on insurance assets while minimizing excessive risk. If maintaining that spread becomes more challenging across cycles, the additional value of this segment may be less than expected.

Finally, the market may keep giving Brookfield a structural discount, no matter how well it does in business. Even if earnings from fees and insurance assets grow, the complexity of the balance sheet, the use of leverage, and the ownership of assets may limit how much the valuation multiple grows over time.

Key Takeaway

The Brookfield Corporation is well positioned as a strong compounder for the long haul. Its targets suggest annual core earnings growth of more than 20%, even as high as 25% if carried interest and capital allocation are taken into account, which is quite unusual for companies of such size.

The reliability of that forecast is underpinned by the profit-driven asset management and the fast-growing insurance operations that Brookfield Wealth Solutions engages in. Although challenges exist in the form of real estate risk and structural complexity, the firm’s potential to create recurrent income and allocate capital within the ecosystem is realizable.

data.title: CES Energy Solutions: Moving To A Hold After Triple-Digit Performance
data.description:
Introduction

Back in September, I was still bullish on CES Energy Solutions (CEU:CA) (CESDF), although the share price had already appreciated by quite a bit. The FY 2025 results didn’t disappoint, but as the share price has doubled in the past six months, I wanted to have another look at the stock to see if a buy rating is still appropriate.

This article is meant as an update to previous coverage. To get a better understanding of CES' business model and activities, I’d like to refer you to my September 2025 article.

While the company is, of course, subject to the oil and gas price environment, it has built up a strong position in North American markets. In 2025, it reached a 25% market share in the US drilling fluids sector, while it has a very enviable 41% market share in Canada. Its customers predominantly focus on quality over pricing, and that's why CES has been able to grow its presence in its core markets.

The financial performance remains strong

As highlighted in my previous coverage on CES Energy Solutions, the company’s financial performance was quite strong. Revenue increased by approximately 6% to C$2.5B, but there was some pressure on the margins, and the gross margin increased by just under 2%. And as the G&A expenses increased by about 2% as well, the net impact of the higher revenue on the operating profit was just 1%.

Fortunately, CES Energy reported a substantial reduction in its finance costs. Keep in mind this includes FX fluctuations, so you could argue the finance expenses were unreasonably high in 2024, and we're now seeing a more normalized finance expense. As you can see below, the pure interest expenses were pretty stable at around C$33.5M, so we should also consider the low FY 2025 finance cost to be mainly non-recurring in nature.

This helped CES to post a net profit of just under C$205M for an EPS of C$0.93 (of which C$0.32 was generated in the final quarter of 2025). While that’s a 10% increase compared to the preceding year (on an EPS level, the net income increase was just 7%, but CES Energy has been buying back its own shares, resulting in a stronger per-share performance).

In my previous article I focused on the company’s cash flow performance, as that isolates the non-cash items that could potentially weigh on a bottom line result on an income statement.

As you can see below, CES reported a total FFO of C$324M (this is a non-GAAP metric but provides a good representation of the cash flow result) before taking changes in the working capital position into account. We should also deduct the almost C$44M in lease payments, resulting in an underlying FFO of approximately C$281M.

This was more than sufficient to cover the C$99M in capital expenditures (which were partially offset by the C$10.5M in cash proceeds generated through the sale of assets).

This results in an underlying net free cash flow result of C$182M. Divided over the net share count of 211M shares at the end of 2025 (CES Energy repurchased 16.8M shares at a total cost of C$140M), this represents a net free cash flow result of C$0.86 per share. And while that made the stock attractive when it was trading at a double-digit free cash flow yield, the current share price indicates CES is trading at approximately 20 times its last year's free cash flow result.

CES expects a total capex of approximately C$90M (net of proceeds generated through the disposal of assets) with a 50/50 breakdown between sustaining capex and growth capex. This indeed indicates the maintenance capex will be just around C$50M this year.

The company has not provided full-year revenue or cash flow guidance as the visibility remains a bit limited, but I hope to see additional details when the company publishes its Q1 results.

While it's difficult to project an anticipated growth rate in the future, as there are a lot of variables at play here (with the oil price and development activities as key drivers), CES Energy Solutions has been able to grow its revenue at a mid- to high single-digit percentage in the past, and I think it's fair to assume we will continue to see a similar growth rate. I'm expecting a slower growth in market share, and I hope the company's price setting will gain strength, as that's the key path forward toward revenue growth, EBITDA growth, and net profit and free cash flow growth.

Investment thesis

CES Energy Solutions continued to buy back its own shares in the first few months of 2026 as the company spent almost C$20M on buying back 1.28M shares at an average price of C$13 per share. I do hope the company has throttled back its share buyback pace, as it makes less sense to buy back stock at C$17 than it did at C$8 or even C$10.

CES Energy Solutions is an attractive company with a solid business model. But as the stock is trading at almost 18 times this year’s consensus earnings estimate, I think a "hold" rating is more appropriate. Although we have seen a multiple expansion throughout the sector, a portion of this expansion was likely fueled by an improving macro environment, but a normalization of the oil and gas price will likely result in a corresponding normalization when it comes to the earnings multiple. I don't necessarily expect the stock to go back to its single-digit earnings multiple, but a multiple of 12-15 times the earnings seems reasonable. Leaning toward the higher end of that range (given CES' strong revenue and market share growth), the current multiple of 18 times earnings appears to be a tad high.

I will continue to track the company’s performance as I consider any strong pullback to be an excellent opportunity to re-initiate a long position.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Umicore: Doubled Since I Called The Bottom, What Now?
data.description:
Umicore S.A. (UMICF) (UMICY) is a materials technology and recycling company based in Belgium. The company traces its roots back 200 years, when it started life as a smelting company, quickly diversifying into mining. At the beginning of the century, Umicore made a strategic pivot to align with the megatrends of electrification, battery technology, and the circular economy. Their major business today is as a manufacturer and recycler of catalysts and battery materials for use in the automotive sector.

It is organized today into four major business groups. Batteries, Catalysis, Specialty Materials, and Recycling.

In a world that is moving rapidly towards emissions control and electrification, these are all strategic growth opportunities.

However, significant investment is required to build capacity, and demand has been inconsistent. Nevertheless, in the long run, demand growth for Umicore's products remains inevitable. EV adoption and other battery-powered demand are expected to rise exponentially, and tailwinds for catalytic and cobalt demand are also projected.

I last covered Umicore nearly a year ago in Umicore: Searching For the Elusive Bottom. I rated the stock a Buy, calling for 40% upside to materialise over several quarters.

As it turns out, I underestimated the power of the recovery. The share price hit 40% gains by midyear and registered upside of 150% in early 2026, only to fall back in the recent team Iran War market volatility.

The price now sits about 2x the May bottom. Umicore reported FY 2025 results in February. This provides a good basis to reassess the investment thesis.

FY 2025 Results

The reported numbers were solid. Revenues grew by a modest 3%, and an improvement in margins from 22% EBITDA to 24% EBITDA delivered an 11% year-on-year EBITDA improvement. While this is a nice print, it needs to be considered in the context that the prior FY registered a 20% drop in EBITDA on the back of an 11% drop in revenues. (The FY 2024 figures are discussed in my previous article.)

The leverage ratio dropped to 1.6x EBITDA as the company continued to allocate capital conservatively.

Free cash flow of EUR 524m was a strong improvement on the prior year's EUR 384m.

The P&L turned around after the huge 2024 battery metals impairment. While adjusted EPS grew from EUR 1.06 to EUR 1.20, the unadjusted earnings went from a negative EUR 1.48bn to a positive EUR 385m.

Segment Analysis

The Battery Metals segment continued to plug away at returning to profit. The adjusted EBITDA for the segment managed to deliver break-even in 2025.

In the Catalysis segment, the company is facing a structural demand shift, as the number of new ICE engine vehicles gradually continues to decline. Revenues were consequently flat, but cost discipline allowed Umicore to grow EBITDA by a modest 4.4%.

The Recycling segment performed well on the back of record prices for platinum and other precious metals in 2025. However, the benefits to the bottom line were limited by hedging costs.

To stabilise earnings and credit ratings, Umicore hedges precious metals. While this generates earnings stability, it does act to cap upside.

Despite this, the Umicore share price has a very high correlation with precious metals prices. Look, for example, at the correlation to platinum below. The market seems to miss that Umicore actually hedges away much of the upside. I will expand on this point below.

Finally, in the Specialty Materials segment, both revenues and margins expanded, allowing an 11% improvement in year-on-year EBITDA contribution.

Hedging Costs

Investors who look under the hood might be disappointed (and surprised) to see how the hedging costs negate precious metals price gains from flowing through to the bottom line.

You can see the impact clearly in the EBITDA bridge slide below. Precious metals pricing contribution after hedging was a negative EUR 17m. The positive results momentum was from cost efficiencies and operating margin improvements.

The problem here is the huge volatility premiums associated with such a rapid increase in the price of precious metals. In 2025, this was enough to leverage futures and options prices in PMs enough that the implied volatility drove hedge prices up faster than gains on outward metals sales.

In a falling precious metals price market, Umicore margins will be protected in the short term by hedges, but they can't book excess gains in a rapidly rising market. For precious metals prices to really create value for the Umicore bottom line, the optimal market environment would be a slow and steady rise in PM prices.

Hedging is undertaken for 2-plus years in advance, with most metals hedged already out to 2028.

Guidance

The company provided guidance for 2025, given the very challenging 2024 results. For 2026, however, they reverted to their prior policy of not providing guidance. Their outlook for 2026 was broadly positive; however, they were looking for costs to increase somewhat on the back of the EUR 100m savings in 2025.

There's not much to support a forward estimate of earnings here or provided on the conference call.

Valuation

Since my last article, Umicore has benefitted from both earnings improvement and multiple re-ratings. A year ago, the stock was priced at just 7.3x FCF and 9x earnings. The valuation has now increased to 11.3x earnings and 14.4x FCF.

Peers in the technology materials segment, catalytics and specialty minerals, include Solvay (SVYSF) and Arkema (ARKAY). This seems to trade in a long-term price-to-FCF range of 7x to 14x.

Conclusion

Umicore had a terrible 2024 performance year, in which it was forced to take a huge write-down on its battery metals investments to service the North American market.

In 2025 the company staged an impressive turnaround, issuing and delivering on cost-cutting and earnings targets and improving margins. Revenue growth was, however, modest.

The stock price has improved dramatically along with the underlying performance. It shows a very strong correlation with precious metals prices, despite the upside and downside commodity price risk being hedged out to 2028.

Meanwhile, the market valuation on earnings and FCF has increased to peer group multiples.

The management gives little guidance for 2026; however, the cost reduction tailwind is now in the rearview mirror, and costs and capex are expected to increase. I expect to see top line growth and some margin compression to follow.

The company segments sit across a slightly declining Catalytics demand and a fast growing battery metals, recycling, and specialty materials demand. In the long term I expect to see high single digit FCF and earnings growth and an FCF multiple in the 10-15% range.

At current pricing, I am re-rating Umicore to a Hold. However, with the strong correlation to precious metals, share price volatility can be expected. I'd be adding on dips to the lower end of the price to FCF range indicated above, say below $17.50 for UMICF.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lansing Street Advisors Q1 2026 Letter
data.description:
All Along the Watchtower

Song by The Jimi Hendrix Experience (1968)

“There must be some kind of way out of here”

Said the joker to the thief

“There’s too much confusion I can’t get no relief

Businessmen, they drink my wine Plowmen dig my earth

None will level on the line

Nobody offered his word”

Introduction

“There’s too much confusion, I can’t get no relief.” Hendrix wasn’t writing about global markets, but he might as well have been. War in Iran, political turmoil, an AI reckoning, and a private credit crisis are dominating headlines all at once and investors are searching for a way out. But through the noise, American business keeps doing what it does best: innovating and producing higher earnings.

In my year-end 2025 letter, we discussed stock market volatility versus bear markets, with the expectation that 2026 would bring heightened turbulence. That expectation has arrived in force. Although the Iran conflict is front and center, the broader thesis from that letter remains intact. As the chart below shows, 100% of midterm election years since 1950 have produced significant corrections, averaging more than –17%.

Add the well-documented pattern of –10%+ pullbacks surrounding the appointment of a new Fed chair, and 2026 is delivering both events in a single year.

The good news: one-year returns following midterm correction troughs have been positive every single time.

The year-end letter also introduced “the law of unpredictability”—which turned out to be accidentally perfect timing heading into the 2026 bombing of Iran. Markets are amoral. Wars tend to produce short-term disruptions that, historically, create buying opportunities. As the chart below illustrates, geopolitical shocks have rarely derailed markets for long. That may prove true again—but today’s letter will examine the three trends I believe will define the balance of 2026.

Part I. Rising Prices and Rising Rates After the Bombs Drop

The Iran conflict triggered the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, and that single event sent commodities sharply higher. Oil grabbed the headlines, but other critical supply chains—shipping, fertilizer, chemicals—have also been disrupted. Just when the Fed appeared ready to cut rates, a geopolitical curveball sent interest rates in the opposite direction.

The charts below paint a picture of rising prices hitting Americans where it hurts most. This comes at a time when affordability is the number one political topic in the country—and more importantly, it is pushing the 10- year Treasury yield higher.

Shipping, Fertilizer, and Chemical Price Increases

Right now, Americans are feeling the pain at the gas pump, in the grocery store, and through higher borrowing costs. But international markets are in a full-blown supply chain crisis. Unleaded gas is up roughly 35%.

Fertilizer and shipping costs are driving food prices higher. Mortgage rates remain stubbornly elevated.

For all you Millennials and Gen Z readers—do not look now, but energy stocks are outperforming technology stocks on a one-year basis. As the chart below shows, energy is closing in on three-year outperformance. This may dissipate quickly if the conflict resolves, but oil prices are unlikely to reset to recent lows under any uneasy peace agreement.

XLE Energy ETF (Purple) vs. XLK Tech ETF (Orange)

The biggest market consequence of the war is that the Fed is now frozen, with almost no chance of rate cuts in 2026. In fact, as the chart below shows, there is now a higher probability of a rate hike than a cut.

Probability of a Rate Hike Higher Than a Cut (as of March 2026)

Part II. Private Credit and Private Equity

“There is never just one cockroach.” —Jamie Dimon, JPMorgan

Rising interest rates and a frozen Fed are the perfect segue into a more dangerous market condition: the private credit and private equity sector. These industries experienced explosive growth coming out of the 2008 financial crisis, stepping in where banks pulled back from lending to small businesses. They have been one of the great American financial success stories of the last fifteen years. Now they are facing their first real test.

Let me be clear at the outset: Lansing Street Advisors would not exist without private equity. A good portion of our clients built their wealth by selling companies to private equity funds. Private credit and equity have delivered massive liquidity to small business owners across the country while generating strong returns for fund shareholders. But like any sector, they will experience bull and bear markets. This is their first full-blown sector bear market—and the question is how far the damage will spread.

I am going to do my best to keep this at an elevated level and not lose readers in the weeds. Two definitions:

Private credit is debt financing provided directly by non-bank lenders to companies through privately negotiated loans. Think of it as non-bank loans.

Private equity is where funds acquire or take stakes in private companies, improve their operations, and sell them for a profit. Think of it as funds taking equity stakes in small businesses.

Right now, the crisis is concentrated in private lending, but the risk of it spreading to private equity is part of the broader apprehension. According to Pitchbook, private credit has tripled since 2015 to $1.3 trillion. The Financial Times puts total private loans at $2 trillion by the end of 2025.

In 2025, two large defaults—Tricolor Holdings and FirstBrands Group—ignited fear across the sector. Both companies sought bankruptcy protection amid accusations of fraudulent bookkeeping. These cases raised serious red flags about the level of underwriting and due diligence in the private lending industry. As Jamie Dimon has said: “There is never just one cockroach.”

Institutional investors—pension funds, endowments, hedge funds, and insurance companies—own an estimated 70% of private loans, with the remaining 30% held by high-net-worth individuals.

Here is the next key point to understand: private credit is “semi-liquid,” typically capping quarterly withdrawals at 5%. You cannot press a key on your computer and sell your holdings. If withdrawal requests exceed the 5% cap, investors get prorated and must resubmit the following quarter. As the charts below show, withdrawal requests have increased—sparking worries about a classic “run on the bank.”

Speaking of banks: the private lending business has been so lucrative that traditional banks have made roughly $300 billion in loans to private credit companies. Yes, banks lending to companies that lend to companies. The good news is that U.S. bank balance sheets are in solid shape and $300 billion in exposure is manageable—but this daisy chain reinforces the broader credit-crisis narrative.

On top of banks, insurance companies have approximately 25% of their investment portfolios in private lending. Pensions and endowments hold up to 30% in illiquid private equity and debt. Trillions in private funds, public bank exposure, life insurance exposure, institutional money exposure.

This is why the alarming phrase “credit crisis” is back in the headlines. We have not had a credit crisis in the United States since 2008. A credit crisis freezes lending and liquidity across the economy as defaults and loss of confidence feed on each other.

This leads to the big question: what is the current default rate? Fitch reports 9.2% for 2025—high enough to leave investors worried about being the last one out the door. We have wood to chop before this is over.

Exacerbating the problem are three structural factors:

Illiquidity. Publicly traded private funds are semi-liquid with quarterly withdrawal caps. Non-publicly traded funds have no set liquidity dates whatsoever.

Opaqueness. Private managers lack the disclosure requirements of public companies. “Mark-to-market” pricing on loan values and company valuations is self-reported. When you grade your own homework, investors get nervous.

Complexity. Here are just some of the strategies being executed by private equity firms: secondaries, continuation funds, NAV lending, preferred equity, strip sales, sale of GP stakes, and PIK loans. Insurance companies with large private investments run offshore reinsurance affiliates domiciled in Bermuda or the Cayman Islands, adding layers of regulatory complexity.

The final blow to private lending in late 2025 was the repricing of the technology sector—especially what some are calling the “SAAS apocalypse,” where AI tools began disrupting traditional software-as-a-service subscription models that were once considered toll-booth businesses with bulletproof recurring revenue.

Private credit lenders have 25–30% exposure to software companies, and AI’s potential impact on this sub- sector has made investors skeptical that anyone can reliably model future cash flows or valuations. More on this in Part III.

If the private world goes “worst case”—are we facing a 2008 situation?

I think not.

As Torsten Slok at Apollo has pointed out, the direct lending market is roughly $2 trillion—representing about 3% of total debt outstanding for U.S. households and businesses. By comparison, mortgages accounted for approximately 60% of total household and corporate debt at the peak of the housing bubble in 2006. Three percent versus sixty percent. The scale is simply not comparable.

Private equity and credit filled a void of liquidity after the 2008 crisis. Now they are facing their first sector-level credit crunch. This will create winners and losers, but America is a dynamic marketplace—when these loans take bigger markdowns, opportunistic investors will line up to buy them at a discount. That’s how markets work.

The questions remain: how far will this affect the real economy, and will it spread beyond the private sector?

Right now, loan growth at banks remains strong, credit spreads are still tight, and there are no signs of a systemic crisis materializing. But we are in the early innings of a private lending bear market, and prudence is recommended.

Part III. The Revaluation of the Technology Sector

Here is a statistic that would have seemed impossible two years ago: technology stocks’ forward P/E multiples now equal the defensive consumer staples sector. In 2021, the Magnificent 7 traded at roughly 40 times earnings versus 20 times for staples. Today, that gap has closed entirely.

Technology sub-sectors are in bear markets—not corrections. The distinction matters.

Software (IGV): down 33%. Cybersecurity (CIBR): down 20%. SaaS (XSW): down 33%. Magnificent 7 (MAGS): down 17.5%. A number of well-known retail trading favorites in these sub-sectors have been cut in half.

This all started in October 2025 with the beginning of the SaaS and crypto repricing, when AI tools demonstrated their potential as a genuine threat to the software-as-a-service subscription model. At the same time, the market grew skeptical of hyper-scalers’ massive AI spending without evidence of immediate revenue growth.

AI remains a “known unknown.” The market does not yet know who the winners and losers will be. But rather than wait around to find out, investors have chosen to de-risk first and ask questions later. That de-risking has played out as a broad technology trading slump—and it feeds directly into the private credit problem discussed in Part II, where lenders have heavy exposure to the very software companies being repriced.

Conclusion

The S&P 500 has compounded at a mid-teens annual rate for the 17 years from the trough of March 2009 through the close of 2025. In that period, the world witnessed roughly 200 conflicts and 10 to 15 major wars.

As the chart below shows, the S&P 500 has delivered a positive one-year return following virtually every major geopolitical event in modern history.

Right now, I rank the Iran war as #3 on the 2026 risk scale—behind the private equity/credit bear market at #2 and the “known unknown” of the AI buildout at #1. We expect volatility to remain elevated through 2026 due to midterm elections and the new Fed chair appointment.

Hendrix sang about confusion, about businessmen drinking his wine, about no one leveling on the line. That feels about right for 2026. There is genuine uncertainty in this market—more than we’ve seen in years. A war nobody predicted, a credit cycle nobody modeled, and a technology repricing nobody timed.

But confusion is not the same thing as catastrophe. And volatility is not the same thing as a bear market. Those distinctions matter—and understanding them is what separates investors from speculators.

The two riders approaching in Hendrix’s song could be anything—fear and opportunity, risk and reward, panic and patience. History tells us the investors who stay the course through periods of maximum confusion are the ones who come out ahead.

As always, stay the course. Investing is a psychology game, not an IQ game.

All Along the Watchtower

Song by The Jimi Hendrix Experience (1968)

All along the watchtower Princes kept the view

While all the women came and went Barefoot servants too

Outside in the cold distance A wildcat did growl

Two riders were approaching And the wind began to howl

Disclosures

Lansing Street Advisors, LLC is a registered investment advisor with the State of Pennsylvania. You can read more about the Lansing team at www.lansingadv.com.

To the extent that content includes references to securities, those references do not constitute an offer or solicitation to buy, sell or hold such security as information is provided for educational purposes only. Articles should not be considered investment advice and the information contained within should not be relied upon in assessing whether to invest in any securities or asset classes mentioned. Please keep in mind that a company’s past financial performance, including the performance of its share price, does not guarantee future results.
