data.title: Intel Proved Me Wrong - Now It Has To Prove The Market Right (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
When I last covered Intel Corporation (INTC) in late January of this year, I said the 13% pullback wasn’t a buy signal. In retrospect, I was wrong, as the stock is up over 33% since, outperforming the market, with the S&P 500 (SP500) actually down around 1.3% during the same period.

During this broader market meltdown as a result of the geopolitical uncertainty and supply chain disruptions, it seems as though Intel is holding up better than most. The stock is up around 34% over the past month, and up another 69% year to date. There aren't many names in tech that can make that claim right now. The semis, broadly, are getting worked, as TSMC (TSM) is navigating Taiwan Strait anxiety on top of the Hormuz supply chain disruption. Nvidia (NVDA) is caught between insatiable AI infrastructure demand and the uncomfortable reality that its most important customers, AKA hyperscalers building out Gulf-region data centers, are now operating in a war zone, and the list goes on.

So, Intel’s outperformance is a function of what Intel isn't: it isn't a pure-play AI accelerator story dependent on uninterrupted Pacific shipping lanes, nor is it priced to perfection. In a market that is actively repricing geopolitical concentration risk, Intel’s U.S. manufacturing footprint is suddenly looking less like a liability and more like a strategic asset. The irony is not lost on anyone who spent the last three years watching the market punish Intel for being too slow, too domestic, and too everything that TSMC wasn't.

Fund managers who spent Q1 overweight on TSMC and Nvidia are now sitting on geopolitical concentration risk they didn't model for, and Intel is one of the few names in the sector where that conversation doesn't apply, since you don't have to worry about a Taiwan Strait incident taking out your fab. Still, Intel continues doing the hard, slow, expensive work of rebuilding a manufacturing operation that should never have been allowed to fall behind in the first place, which makes me think that this run is standing on shaky grounds.

So, the question remains: why is Intel up 30% when the peers are struggling to have a green day?

Intel has spent the last six weeks quietly assembling a set of catalysts that nobody had fully priced in January. They next report their earnings on April 23rd. So, here's the rundown:

Why the market (suddenly) loves Intel:

Let’s start with April 1st. Intel announced it would repurchase Apollo Global Management's 49% equity stake in its Fab 34 facility in Leixlip, Ireland, for $14.2 billion, the same stake Apollo had originally acquired for $11.2 billion in 2024 when Intel needed outside capital to survive. The market took this positively, and rightfully so, as the stock jumped around 9% on the day, because a company buying back a strategic fab at a 27% premium to what it sold it for is not a company in distress. According to Intel’s CFO, David Zinser,

“Today, we have a stronger balance sheet, improved financial discipline, and an evolved business strategy. We appreciate Apollo’s continued collaboration to reach this outcome as we realign our capital structure with our long-term strategy.”

Then came Terafab. On April 7th, Intel announced it would partner with SpaceX (SPACE), Tesla (TSLA), and xAI (X.AI) on the Terafab project, a mega-fab in Austin designed to produce 1 terawatt of AI compute per year, with small-batch production expected in late 2026. According to Intel in an X post,

"Our ability to design, fabricate, and package ultra-high-performance chips at scale will help accelerate Terafab’s aim to produce 1 TW/year [terawatt per year] of compute to power future advances in AI and robotics.”

Having Musk's full ecosystem as a manufacturing customer provides something money cannot buy, which is basically reputational proof that Intel Foundry is a serious alternative to TSMC for cutting-edge AI silicon, or at least getting there. The stock was up another 3% on the announcement.

Then comes Google (GOOG)(GOOG). Intel deepened its partnership with Google to co-develop custom ASIC-based infrastructure processing units. According to Amin Vahdat, SVP & Chief Technologist, AI Infrastructure at Google,

“Intel has been a trusted partner for nearly two decades, and their Xeon roadmap gives us confidence that we can continue to meet the growing performance and efficiency demands of our workloads.”

The Google partnership is the one I am watching. Terafab is flashy and is a bet on the future, but Google is the present. Let’s think about what Google is telling its own procurement team with that statement. It's saying, we're comfortable committing to Intel's CPU roadmap for multiple generations of AI infrastructure build-out.

In an environment where hyperscalers are simultaneously building custom ASICs and AI accelerators specifically to reduce dependence on any single vendor, choosing to publicly reaffirm a multi-generational commitment to Intel's server roadmap is a meaningful signal, and the market took it as such, with the stock up another 5% on the news. This means the Xeon 6 is earning its place in Google's AI stack on performance merits.

Investment thesis:

When I said in January that the pullback wasn't a buy signal, I was looking at a company still burning cash on a foundry build, and still behind on process nodes, while sentiment ran through the roof, and most importantly, valuation ran ahead of fundamentals.

My argument for the sell was simple: the fundamentals hadn't changed; the market had just run too far ahead of them. The government taking a 10% stake in August had lit a fuse under the stock, and what I was watching for was real evidence. At the time, my takeaway was the following:

“What this quarter reinforced for me is that Intel’s story is still one of transition. There is real progress in data centers and AI, and the foundry narrative is directionally positive, but neither is yet strong enough to offset persistent weakness in PCs and margin compression. Soft guidance was the trigger, but the selloff was ultimately about valuation and positioning, not one quarter’s numbers. Intel continues to lose market share to AMD, and Panther Lake, though impressive, does not change the competitive reality in the near term. I think the stock will offer more attractive entry points in the mid-term, and would recommend investors to stay on the sidelines.”

Still, looking back, what I wasn't modeling was the velocity of the narrative shift. The geopolitical environment handed Intel a structural argument, onshore fabs, no Taiwan risk, CHIPS Act backing, and amid all that, the company didn’t stand still.

The company reports Q1 '26 on April 23rd. Intel guided for revenue of approximately $12.2 billion at the midpoint, an 11% sequential decline, with breakeven adjusted EPS and gross margins around 34.5%. The fundamentals are still messy, and the foundry segment is still unprofitable, but conventional screens aren't what's driving this. I was wrong in January, but the harder question now is whether I’m late to the party. With a lot of the positives priced in, what can really push the stock up from here?

My original thesis was a valuation call, not a fundamentals one, and that trade has played out. Now, while the narrative has shifted, I'm not chasing this at 69% year-to-date with breakeven EPS guidance and earnings carrying real downside risk if gross margins disappoint. The stock is trading well above where most analysts think it should be, but I’m upgrading the stock to a hold at current levels. The story is better than I thought, and the price reflects that. But until earnings later this month, I'm on the sidelines, just for different reasons than before.

Valuation and what’s next:

The stock is currently trading at $63, above all its EMAs, with EMA21 at $50, EMA50 AT $47, and EMA200 at $38. With RSI in overbought territory at 75, I would like to see the stock give back some of its gains, and enter the 1Q print with sentiment and fundamentals more aligned. The ideal entry for anyone not already in this is a pullback toward the $50-52 range, where the EMA21 provides support and the technical picture is less extended. But buying a 75 RSI into a quarter where revenue is expected to decline 11% sequentially is a risk that the risk-reward doesn't justify right now.

Forward P/E is at 125x, against a sector median of 21.6x, and forward EV/Sales is at 6.22x versus a sector median of 2.81x. The only honest way to defend these multiples is to argue that the forward estimates are depressed and will be revised materially upward as the foundry business scales and margins recover, but that's still a faith-based valuation.

Buying Intel at these levels is paying for what Intel might earn two or three years from now if 18A yields improve and 14A lands external customers. To keep a long story short, the margin for error is thin, and a lot is counting on Intel’s next print.
